snippet.title: Everyday Ham
snippet.description:
A couple of Michigan Amateurs getting together monthly to discuss current Ham Radio topics in an approachable, conversational format! We'll share our learning and experience of operating the radio, while talking equipment, modes and preparedness as it intersects in the amateur radio hobby.

Hosted by N8JRD - Jim, K8JKU - James, and Rory - W8KNX

snippet.title: How We Made Field Day Our Best One Yet
snippet.description:
We reinvented Field Day as a community open house, and it might be our favorite one yet. Watch the full South Lyon Field Day walkthrough here: https://youtu.be/hcXOOwiPnr0

In this episode, James (K8JKU), Jim (N8JRD), and Rory (W8KNX) recap a busy July. We chase the 13 Colonies Special Event through a solar storm, dig into Monroe Ham Swap finds, rethink what ARRL Field Day can be, give an honest first look at the Buddipole BuddiHEX hex beam, and preview the Icom ID-5200 versus Kenwood TM-D750A mobile radio showdown.

snippet.title: Is Parks on the Air Past Its Prime? An Honest Debate
snippet.description:
Has Parks on the Air already peaked? This clip is from Everyday Ham Episode 18. Watch the full episode here: https://youtu.be/xKMKYlSsglY?si=StD8OCJ-iOcVTrw6

James (K8JKU), Jim (N8JRD), and Rory (W8KNX) tackle the question every activator is quietly asking: is POTA still growing, or has the program hit its plateau? The guys debate whether Parks on the Air is in its twilight or its golden years, why the growth curve is flattening now that most active hams have already tried it, and what the declining solar cycle means for a program built around QRP and portable operating. They also get into why tougher band conditions may push more operators toward CW and FT8, why bringing every tool in the toolbox matters more than ever, and the volunteers and board members working to keep POTA running for the long haul.

Parks on the Air is a volunteer-run organization. If you love POTA, consider donating at https://parksontheair.com

Watch the full episode, including our recap of the Third Annual Michigan POTA Meetup at Hartwick Pines State Park: https://youtu.be/xKMKYlSsglY?si=StD8OCJ-iOcVTrw6

snippet.title: Field Day 2026 Setup Walkthrough: BuddiHex, BandHopper & Larry the Vertical
snippet.description:
A full walking tour of the South Lyon Area Amateur Radio Club's 2026 Field Day site at the historic Witch's Hat Depot in downtown South Lyon, MI. If you're looking for ideas on how to plan, set up, and run your own Field Day, this one's for you.

We moved Field Day into town this year and built the whole site from scratch, right across from the brewery. In this tour, James (K8JKU), Jim (N8JRD), and Rory (W8KNX) walk you through every decision behind the South Lyon Area Amateur Radio Club (N8SL) operation, a 3A entry plus a Get On The Air station, so you can borrow what works.

What you'll pick up for your own site:
- How we laid out antennas across a tight in-town lot: the homebrew "Larry" 40m vertical, our first hex beam in years (the BuddiHEX), and a Spiderbeam mast stacking the BandHopper 4 on 80m over an Aerial-51 off-center fed dipole
- A beginner-friendly GOTA setup (Icom IC-7300 + a visible, easy-to-adjust Buddistick) with a homemade exchange cheat sheet and paper logging sheet to get first-timers over mic fright
- Keeping operators consistent: a matched fleet of Yaesu FTDX10s on N1MM+, networked with a live projector scoreboard so visitors can follow along
- Cheap, effective site safety and public outreach: caution tape on $2 step-in fence posts, explanatory antenna signs, and a "What is Field Day?" welcome booth
- Small-club realities: crew size, ease-of-setup ratings, and why we finally invested in a directional antenna

- Buddistick PRO vertical (Buddipole): https://www.buddipole.com/buddistick.html
- SOTABeams Band Hopper 4 linked dipole: https://www.sotabeams.co.uk/band-hopper-iv-four-band-20m-30m-40m-80m-portable-dipole-antenna-system/

snippet.title: Raising a BuddiHEX Hex Beam at Field Day 2026 | South Lyon Area ARC
snippet.description:
We'd never built a BuddiHEX before, so we did it live at Field Day. Full first-time assembly here: https://www.buddipole.com/buddihex2.html

Join the Everyday Ham crew and the South Lyon Area Amateur Radio Club (SLAARC) as we unbox and build a Buddipole BuddiHEX 6-band portable hex beam for the very first time during ARRL Field Day 2026, set up at the historic Witch's Hat Depot in Downtown South Lyon, Michigan. This is a long-form club antenna build walkthrough: every wire element, every guy line, the lost hardware in the tall grass, the near-miss with a mast section, and the lessons we learned along the way. If you like honest, in-depth antenna setups from people figuring it out as they go, this one's for you. Final verdict: an 8/10 setup that goes together smoothly once you know the steps, but bring a buddy.

In this video:
Unboxing the BuddiHEX and laying out the pre-tuned wire elements
Setting up the 7m Mastwerks rotating tripod and mast
Leveling the tripod (and when to actually do it)
Attaching the 6 band-specific wire elements to the spreader arms
The lost hook hunt and a re-soldering repair
Guying the mast without snapping a section
Raising the antenna and final thoughts


GEAR MENTIONED
Buddipole BuddiHEX (6-band portable hex beam, 20/17/15/12/10/6m, 9.5 lbs, 1.5 kW, BNC feed, $749): https://www.buddipole.com/buddihex2.html
Buddipole 7m Mastwerks rotating tripod & mast system (23 ft / 7 m, 10.9 lbs, $789): https://www.buddipole.com/7mmatrandmas.html


Huge thanks to every volunteer who showed up. Field Day doesn't happen without you.

snippet.title: What Happens When 80 Hams Finally Meet in Person? Michigan POTA Meetup 2026
snippet.description:
You work the same callsigns for years until they start to feel like family, and the Michigan POTA Meetup is the one day those voices finally become faces.

Everyday Ham: https://www.everydayham.com/

On Saturday, June 6, 2026, somewhere between 60 and 80 hams from across Michigan gathered at Hartwick Pines State Park (US-1503) near Grayling for the 2026 Michigan POTA Meetup. James (K8JKU) and Rory (W8KNX) brought a camera to capture what Parks on the Air looks like in person: homebrew antennas, field stations running hard on a busy band, a bingo game built to get quiet operators talking, and a raffle antenna that got passed forward the moment it was won. We talked with POTA board members and the operators who have years of contacts in their logs without ever meeting face to face, and the same thread kept surfacing on its own. This hobby is about the people.

Chapters:
0:00 Welcome: putting a face to the callsign
1:28 Why everyone gathers
3:23 POTA Bingo
4:40 FT8 on the FT-710
6:05 A classic FT-891 setup
9:52 Homebrew fold-down VHF tower
13:42 The spool antenna giveaway
14:23 Putting a name to the face
16:24 The future of POTA
19:05 Group photo and goodbyes

Featured at the meetup:
POTA board: Mike Case (W8MSC), Tom Martin (W8TAM), Mark Torigian (K8MST)

Local organizer: John (NU8M)

POTA Bingo: Mike (KD8IAK), concept from Mike Turner (W4OPS)

Operators: Leonard (N4LJT), Joe (W8JLN), Ed (W8EDE), Penn (AC8XI), Jim (W8ISS)

Gear seen on camera:
Yaesu FT-710, Yaesu FT-891, Kenwood TS-2000, Yaesu FT-847
ResRanger 280, NFED half-wave spool antenna, vertical dipole, custom stacked VHF tower
FT8 and SSB with WSJT-X, Bioenno battery power

snippet.title: Has POTA Peaked? Campfires, Community, and the Future of Parks on the Air
snippet.description:
Has Parks on the Air hit its peak? In this episode of Everyday Ham, James (K8JKU), Jim (N8JRD), and Rory (W8KNX) dig into the future of POTA, from the program's explosive growth to what the declining solar cycle means for QRP and portable operators, and why CW and FT8 may matter more than ever as band conditions get tougher. Is POTA in its twilight, or just entering its golden years?

The conversation comes fresh off the Third Annual Michigan POTA Meetup, where the guys spent a weekend camping, activating, and swapping stories around the campfire with about 70 Parks on the Air enthusiasts at Hartwick Pines State Park. They share what makes a regional meetup worth the drive, what actually brings hams together, and how you can find or even start a meetup in your own area, wherever you call home.

Also in this episode: a Hamvention attendance stat correction, shout-outs to Penn (AC8XI) and Rob (N1RWJ), a conversation about POTA board member Mark Torigian (K8MST) and the volunteers keeping the program running, and around the shack updates including Jim's Diamond X300 install, Rory's camper radio setup with the Yaesu FT-891 and FT-8900, and James flashing alternate firmware on his FX-4CR.

Big news: the Everyday Ham merch store is finally live! Find hats, mugs, and more at https://www.everydayham.com

0:51 The Everyday Ham Merch Store Is Live

Also in this episode: a Hamvention attendance stat correction, shout-outs to Penn (AC8XI) and Rob (N1RWJ), a conversation about POTA board member Mark Torigian (K8MST) and the volunteers keeping the program running, and around the shack updates including Jim's Diamond X300 install, Rory's camper radio setup with the Yaesu FT-891 and FT-8900, and James flashing alternate firmware on his FX-4CR.

snippet.title: SpoolTenna Ultra Review: Is the Pocket EFHW Worth the Upgrade?
snippet.description:
The SpoolTenna just got pocket-sized. James (K8JKU) takes the new SpoolTenna Ultra (ULTRA-EFHW4010) into the field and compares it head-to-head with the original Parks: same 40-10M end-fed half-wave performance, less than half the weight, and small enough to vanish into a pocket.

It's the same antenna electrically (same 49:1 UNUN, same toroid, same 65 ft of wire), but rebuilt around portability with thinner 26 AWG copper-clad steel wire, a fiberglass barrel, and a thermal-vented backboard for higher duty cycle work. James owns and runs both versions, so this is a real upgrade question, not a spec-sheet read.

Inside this review: what's in the box, the spec differences that actually matter, a teardown peek at the matching network, the weight verified on his own scale, the per-unit SWR field test report, deployment and tear-down in about five minutes, and the all-important pocket test. Includes a chat with Brian Young (AJ9T) of SpoolTenna, filmed at Four Days in May (FDIM).

Is it worth the upgrade over the Parks? Here's James's honest take after using both.

SpoolTenna Parks: https://www.spooltenna.com/products/spooltenna

snippet.title: Hamvention 2026: Recap, Reactions, and a Brewing Showdown
snippet.description:
The dust has settled on Dayton Hamvention 2026, and the crew is back at the table to break it all down. James (K8JKU) holds down the home front while Jim (N8JRD) and Rory (W8KNX) recap three days of new gear, new conversations, and one very controversial concept radio.
We dig into what Icom, Yaesu, and Kenwood actually brought to Xenia, why the Icom X-026 mockup divided the community, and whether the long-awaited Kenwood TM-D750A is finally close to hitting the shelves. Plus our honest reactions to the Icom ID-5200, a walk through Four Days in May, and a full report from the flea market (yes, the junk scale is back).

🎙️ In this episode
Hamvention 2026 attendance, vibes, and the cooler weather we actually got. Big thanks to Ray (N9JA) at Icom, John Kruk (N9UPC) at Yaesu, and Don Arnold (W6GPS) at Kenwood for the time and conversations.
The Icom X-026 concept reveal: is it brilliant marketing or a warm helping of nothing? Plus hands-on reactions to the Icom ID-5200 with native APRS, ambient light sensor, integrated GPS, and an interface borrowed from the IC-7300 MK2.
A Kenwood TM-D750A update: FCC approved, firmware still cooking, September target. And a real second-half-of-2026 showdown brewing between the ID-5200 and the TM-D750A.
Four Days in May highlights, including the new Spooltenna Ultra EFHW4010. Plus Begali keys, N3ZN keys, Modern Morse paddles, and the CW corner of the show.
Yaesu DR-3 repeater series and the next chapter of System Fusion. Flex Radio Aurora AU-510 and AU-520 spotted on the shelf, ready to walk out.
Flea market report: the junk scale, pie-in-the-sky pricing, and the same FTM-10R that didn't sell last year. And a massive thank you to James for editing eight Hamvention videos remotely via Starlink.

snippet.title: Icom's "Concept Car" - The X-026 with Ray Novak N9JA | Hamvention 2026
snippet.description:
The Icom X-026 had Hamvention buzzing, and we caught up with Ray Novak, N9JA, Senior Sales Manager at Icom America, to find out what's really going on with it.

Here's our take at Everyday Ham: the X-026 reminds us of a concept car at an auto show. Icom didn't call it that, but the spirit is the same. They're showing a direction, gathering feedback, and listening to what operators actually want before committing to a final design. That's a refreshing approach, and Ray walked W8KNX through what they're hearing from the community so far.
In this conversation, Ray shares the top three things hams keep asking Icom to build, addresses some of the online speculation directly, and gives us a sense of when we might learn more.

Spoiler: most of the "X-026 images" you've seen online aren't from Icom. Ray explains.

snippet.title: Hands All Over the Icom ID-5200! | Hamvention 2026
snippet.description:
We're at the Icom America booth at Hamvention 2026 for our first hands-on look at the long-awaited Icom ID-5200, the successor to the ID-5100 dual-band D-STAR mobile that's been a staple of the hobby for over a decade.
W8KNX has been running an ID-5100 in both his base and mobile setups for over 10 years, so when Icom let us pull the ID-5200 out from under the glass for the first time, we had a lot to look at. Jim N8JRD takes us around the control head, the main unit, and the ports while we explore the new color touchscreen, the menu system, the band scope, and a few features the 5100 never had.

🔑 What We Found:
🖥 The Display

4.3-inch color touchscreen
Thinner and lighter control head than the ID-5100
Sharp visibility in harsh light (the ID-5100 washed out in the sun)
Color band scope in single-receive mode
Waterfall display with adjacent band scanning

📻 Modes & Reception

Dual-band 2m / 70cm (144 / 430 MHz)
FM + DV (D-STAR Digital Voice)
Broad reception: VHF, UHF, and Airband
Simultaneous dual receive: FM-FM, FM-DV, and DV-DV
Main and sub band selection with two independent dials

⚡ Connectivity (New)

Built-in Wi-Fi
Built-in Bluetooth
Internal gateway with terminal and access point modes
DV repeater monitor function
APRS support planned via firmware update

🔌 Hardware

USB-C port
Memory card slot
Mic port (on the body, not the side of the head)
GPS receiver on top of the main unit
Speech guidance
Menu system styled like the IC-7300 Mark II and IC-9700

📍 Status: FCC approval pending. No release date announced yet.

snippet.title: It's REAL and It Works! Kenwood TM-D750A First Powered-On Look | Hamvention 2026
snippet.description:
W8KNX stops by the Kenwood booth at Hamvention 2026 to talk with Don Arnold, W6GPS, about the long-awaited Kenwood TM-D750A tri-band mobile radio. After a no-show at HamCation a few months back, Kenwood arrived at Dayton with Serial #1, a fully functioning, powered-on unit on display, receiving live APRS beacons (including from Rory's own D74 on the way in).
Then Jim N8JRD takes us behind the glass for a walkaround of the display case to look at the screen, ports, and microphone up close.

🔑 Confirmed at the Booth:
📡 Kenwood TM-D750A Tri-Band Mobile
2m / 1.25m / 70cm (144 / 222 / 430 MHz)
50W on VHF and UHF, 20W on 220 MHz
Three power settings per band
Single antenna port
Front-firing audio with rear speaker output (and control head speaker output)
Traditional Kenwood mobile-style mic with 10-key DTMF + A/B/C/D

🖥 The Display
3.45-inch TFT color LCD with high brightness and wide viewing angles
Soft keys around the display edge that change with menu context
Clock/data row at the top showing APRS activity
Familiar menu structure for TH-D74 and TH-D75 users
FM-side buttons that mirror the TM-D710

⚡ Quick-Access Features
Dedicated APRS button — one press jumps straight to APRS mode (no menu diving)
Built-in APRS TNC, D-STAR, and GPS

🗓 Release: September 2026 (firmware finalized)

snippet.title: Yaesu DR-3X Revealed! John Kruk N9UPC Walks Us Through System Fusion 3
snippet.description:
We're in the Yaesu booth at Hamvention 2026 and sit down with John Kruk, N9UPC, National Sales Manager of the Amateur Division at Yaesu USA, for a first look at the brand new DR-3X repeater. It kicks off the third generation of Yaesu System Fusion, and John walks us through everything that's changed from the DR-1X and DR-2X.

🔑 What's New with the DR-3X:
📡 Two single-band transmit models with dual-band receive:

DR-3XV (VHF TX): 85W at 100% continuous duty cycle
DR-3XU (UHF TX): 65W at 100% continuous duty cycle (coming)
Both versions can still receive VHF AND UHF

🔌 Full Power on AC or DC

No more dropping to 25W on backup battery power
Run on DC at full output if AC goes out

📻 Yaesu System Fusion 3

Still C4FM / FDMA digital voice
Still AMS (Automatic Mode Select) for analog & digital
Fusion protocol unchanged: existing radios (FT1D, FTM-510, 310, 150, 5 series, FTX-1 and more) still work

🎚 ASP & SDX Now in a Repeater

Audio Signal Processing and SDX brought over from the FTM-510 / 710 series
Increases receiver sensitivity and cleans up analog signals

🛡 IES (Integrated Elimination Services) "Evasion Mode"

Repeater senses interference on the input frequency
Automatically moves to an alternate input and notifies your radio
Works on BOTH analog and digital
Returns to normal once interference clears
Requires firmware update on FTM-510, 310, 150, and 5 series radios

🖥 New Hardware

Full touchscreen with function menu button system
USB-C ports on front AND back
15-pin D-Sub connector
SD card slot (more details coming)
Two RX antenna ports + dedicated TX port
Small rack mount design

🔗 Yaesu Repeater Lineup

snippet.title: Hamvention 2026 Survival Guide: Strategy, Gear & The ICOM X-026 Reveal
snippet.description:
The fastest way to waste Hamvention is to treat it like a random walk. We're getting ahead of Dayton with the game plan we wish we had on day one: how to pace the weekend, when to hit the flea market, how to dodge the worst lines, and why the forums can be the most underrated part of the whole show.

We also catch up from our shacks, because real ham radio life is never just shopping. Rory shares lessons from the Michigan QSO Party and why CW contesting is the best training ground for finally hearing callsigns at speed. James brings the portable angle with travel radio, Parks on the Air stories from Hawaii and the Grand Canyon, and what it takes to make a station work when you're far from home. Jim talks simple antenna building with a 9:1 end-fed and what he's learning by hosting a club System Fusion repeater under his desk.

Then we get into the big Hamvention gear energy: ICOM's X-026 concept has the internet buzzing about a mobile rig that could finally merge HF with VHF/UHF in a clean install. Kenwood's TM-D750A is still the question everyone wants answered, and they've claimed three booths in Building 1 this year. Yaesu staying quiet has us all guessing what comes next for handhelds, battery life, and maybe even a smaller form factor.

If you're heading to Dayton, you'll leave this episode with practical tips you can use immediately and a checklist mindset that keeps the weekend fun. localized.title: Hamvention 2026 Survival Guide: Strategy, Gear & The ICOM X-026 Reveal
localized.description:
The fastest way to waste Hamvention is to treat it like a random walk. We're getting ahead of Dayton with the game plan we wish we had on day one: how to pace the weekend, when to hit the flea market, how to dodge the worst lines, and why the forums can be the most underrated part of the whole show.

We also catch up from our shacks, because real ham radio life is never just shopping. Rory shares lessons from the Michigan QSO Party and why CW contesting is the best training ground for finally hearing callsigns at speed. James brings the portable angle with travel radio, Parks on the Air stories from Hawaii and the Grand Canyon, and what it takes to make a station work when you're far from home. Jim talks simple antenna building with a 9:1 end-fed and what he's learning by hosting a club System Fusion repeater under his desk.

Then we get into the big Hamvention gear energy: ICOM's X-026 concept has the internet buzzing about a mobile rig that could finally merge HF with VHF/UHF in a clean install. Kenwood's TM-D750A is still the question everyone wants answered, and they've claimed three booths in Building 1 this year. Yaesu staying quiet has us all guessing what comes next for handhelds, battery life, and maybe even a smaller form factor.

snippet.title: Returning to the Moon: Optimism & Technology that Moves Our Space Needle
snippet.description:
We take a beat to discuss the recent Artemis II mission, the technology, and the optimism and excitement about sending humans back to the Moon after 50 years! This time on the Everyday Ham monthly podcast, we're discussing the technology, communications, and general mission firsts that make humanity's return to deep space a launching point for future scientific exploration. 

Join us for a lighter topic this month and leave your thoughts on NASA's Artemis II in the comments below!

snippet.title: Is BG2FX Building a New 50-Watt Radio? Life after the FX-4CR is QRO!
snippet.description:
BG2FX, the maker of the popular FX-4CR, has been sharing prototype photos, CAD drawings, and bench test results to his private Facebook group, and we went full speculation mode.
James (K8JKU), Jim (N8JRD), and Rory (W8KNX) dig into everything BG2FX has posted so far, including side-by-side size comparisons to the FX-4CR, a bench test showing 60+ watts output, and 3D-printed prototype shots. We break it all down and share our thoughts on what this new radio could mean for the ultra-portable HF market.

0:00 - Introduction
0:28 - Who is BG2FX and What is the FX-4CR?
2:05 - What Has BG2FX Posted So Far?
3:20 - Size Comparison: FX-4CR vs. the New Radio
4:11 - Walking Through the Prototype Images
5:04 - Power Questions: Can a Barrel Connector Handle 50 Watts?
5:54 - Connector History and Current Draw Discussion
7:09 - Front Panel, Screen, and Display Analysis
13:10 - Button Design and the New Encoder Dial
15:40 - Cooling, Heat Sink, and Thinness of the New Design
17:36 - I/O Section and Second USB-C Speculation
22:05 - Who is This Radio For?
22:47 - Could This Be a G90 Competitor?
23:18 - Pricing Speculation
24:18 - Dual USB-C and Dev Board Details
25:46 - Final Thoughts and Wrap-Up

DISCLAIMER:
Everything discussed is based on developer updates posted by BG2FX. 
No official name, pricing, or release date has been announced.
This is NOT a sponsored video.

snippet.title: Ham2K PoLo Cloud Sync Is Live and It's Just the Beginning
snippet.description:
Ham2K LoFi is here! The long-awaited cloud sync service for PoLo 
just launched, and we sat down with the creator himself to talk 
about it. Sebastián Delmont KI2D joins James, Jim, and Rory for 
a deep dive into what Ham2K LoFi does today and where it is headed.

Topics include syncing logs across multiple devices, a web browser 
log viewer in development, collaborative multi-op logging, direct 
log push to POTA and QRZ, QSO party support, and Sebastián's 
vision for the future of Ham2K as a full logging ecosystem.

If you use PoLo for POTA, SOTA, QSO parties, or everyday logging, 
this one is for you.

Support Sebastián and the Ham2K project:
☕ https://www.buymeacoffee.com/ham2k
📱 Apple App Store: https://apps.apple.com/us/app/ham2k-portable-logger/id6478713938
📱 Google Play: https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.ham2k.polo.beta

🌐 Ham2K: https://www.ham2k.com
🌐 PoLo: https://polo.ham2k.com
📖 PoLo Docs: https://polo.ham2k.com/docs/
▶️ YouTube 🔴https://www.youtube.com/@Ham2KApps

02:35 - Platform support: iOS, Android, and M1/M2 Mac

08:33 - QSO party support in PoLo for 2026

snippet.title: POTA Roving Florida's Space Coast After Hamcation - Seeing NASA Artemis II Moon Rocket
snippet.description:
James (K8JKU), Jim (N8JRD), and Rory (W8KNX) weren't ready to head home after Hamcation in Orlando, so we turned the drive to the airport into a five-park POTA rove along Florida's Space Coast. We kicked things off with a look at our portable load out before hitting the road and making contacts at every stop.

Index of Stuff:
00:00 - Intro and Jeep Loading
00:34 - POTA Gear Load for Flying Spirit Air
03:37 - Jeepin' to the Space Coast
04:26 - First Looks at the ARTEMIS Moon Rocket
04:38 - Picking up Maps (Visitor Center is Closed!)
05:25 - Landing in the First Two Pota Parks
06:21 - Mobile Deployment of the POTA Gear
07:45 - W8MCV QSO
08:43 - Visiting NASA
09:06 - Jeep Stuff
10:05 - Arrival at Park 3/4
13:11 -  Park 5 in the Smoke!
13:48 - 73!

📻 Parks Activated:
US-6329 Tosohatchee Wildlife Management Area
US-6324 Split Oak Forest Wildlife Area
US-4559 Florida Trail National Scenic Trail
US-0660 Canaveral National Seashore
US-0249 Merritt Island National Wildlife Refuge

🎒 POTA Load Out:
Yaesu FT-891
PackTenna
ABR Industries Coax
GigaParts Explorer POTA20

We operated as a multi-op using four callsigns: K8JKU, N8JRD, W8KNX, and the club call W8EDH. Between activations we took in some incredible sights including the Artemis rocket on the launch pad and the Kennedy Space Center.

snippet.title: Are Radio Prices Going Up? FT2, Bouvet 3Y0K, Yaesu & HamClock
snippet.description:
Are radio prices going up? Yes, and we have the numbers. James (K8JKU), Jim (N8JRD), and Rory (W8KNX) dig into Yaesu's incoming price increases, the historic 3Y0K Bouvet Island DXpedition, and what's next for HamClock in Episode 15 of the Everyday Ham Podcast.

📻 Topics in This Episode:
Around the Shack - Bird 43 wattmeter score at the Livonia swap, new Radtel RT-910B and Quansheng UV-K1 handhelds, Astron power supply in the shack, QSO party CW progress, and FT2 mode first impressions
Bouvet Island / 3Y0K DXpedition - The most expensive DXpedition ever is on the air! We talk pile up chaos, FT8 band conditions, operating strategy, and what makes this one historic

Yaesu Price Increases - Big MSRP jumps hit the FTDX10, FT-891, Yaesu Optima, and FTDX101MP. We break down the reasons (tariffs, manufacturing costs, yen/dollar exchange), compare to ICOM pricing, and share our take on whether to buy now or wait
HamClock Alternatives - Three options keeping HamClock alive: hamclock.com (W4BAE), openhamclock.com, and hamvision.net. Plus a shout-out to the Geochron for those who want a turnkey solution
ARRL Win - Amateur operators exempted from foreign adversary contact reporting requirements

snippet.title: Are Radio Prices Going Up? Bouvet, Yaesu & HamClock
snippet.description:
Send us Fan Mail (https://www.buzzsprout.com/2438895/fan_mail/new) 


A Bird 43 wattmeter score at the swap, two new budget handhelds worth a look, and Rory's CW skills getting sharper through QSO parties and daily POTA hunting set the stage for a packed Episode 15.


FT2 mode is here and it is fast - we're talking 3.75-second transmit/receive cycles and roughly 240 QSOs per hour at peak. But speed costs you something: the weak-signal performance drops significantly compared to FT8, and your time sync has to be nearly perfect. We map out where FT2 makes sense and when you should stick with what works.


Then we head south - way south - to Bouvet Island. The 3Y0K DXpedition has the bands blazing and livestreams running via Starlink. But not every station should be in that pile. We talk strategy and sportsmanship: use PSK Reporter or GridTracker to confirm your signal is actually going that direction before calling, understand how Fox and Hound works, and know when your antenna and geography just are not going to win the slot.


The market news is hard to ignore: significant Yaesu MSRP increases across popular rigs including the FTDX10, FT-891, and FTDX101MP. We break down why tariffs, logistics, component costs, and yen-to-dollar swings are all pushing prices up - and whether waiting for Hamvention deals still makes sense.


Finally, HamClock lives on. After the passing of its creator Elwood, the community moved fast. Hamclock.com (W4BAE) restores the data feeds with redundancy and a simple host file edit. OpenHamClock offers a polished browser-based and self-hosted option. HamVision is an emerging alternative worth watching. And Geochron remains a solid turnkey choice for those who want it to just work.


We wrap with a genuine win from the ARRL: amateur operators are exempted from foreign adversary contact reporting requirements. When the community organizes, good things happen.

snippet.title: Do You Really Need a New Radio? Honest Ham Radio Upgrade Advice
snippet.description:
Do you really need that new radio? In Episode 14 of the Everyday Ham Podcast, James (K8JKU), Jim (N8JRD), and Rory (W8KNX) get honest about the pressure to upgrade ham radio equipment — and what they’ve learned from their own buying mistakes.

We start with a moment of remembrance for Elwood Downey, creator of the beloved HamClock, and share what his work meant to the amateur radio community.

18:41 Why antenna upgrades beat radio upgrades every time

snippet.title: Do You Really Need a New Radio? Honest Ham Radio Upgrade Advice
snippet.description:
Send us Fan Mail (https://www.buzzsprout.com/2438895/fan_mail/new) 


Do you really need that new radio? In Episode 14, James (K8JKU), Jim (N8JRD), and Rory (W8KNX) get honest about the pressure to upgrade ham radio gear and share what they’ve learned from their own buying mistakes.


We begin with a moment of remembrance for Elwood Downey, creator of HamClock, and share what his contributions meant to the community. Then we catch up around the shack — Jim’s new CW trainer, James’s hotel radio experiments from Germany, and Rory’s progress with Morse code through the Long Island CW Club.


The main topic digs into upgrade pressure. Jim confesses his $10K shack might not outperform his old $890 FT-DX10 and $99 end-fed combo. Rory makes the case that antennas matter more than radios. James shares his FTX-1F disappointment and why he went crawling back to his trusted FT-891. The unanimous takeaway: more antenna, less radio.


We also talk BuddiHEX plans, hamfest buying strategies heading into Hamcation and Hamvention season, and preview our upcoming trip to Orlando.

snippet.title: Brutal Cold: Winter Field Day at -17°F - Our 2026 WFD Experience | W8EDH
snippet.description:
What happens when you try to operate ham radio during one of the coldest winter weekends Michigan has seen in years? We found out!

Join James (K8JKU), Jim (N8JRD), and Rory (W8KNX) as we recap our 2026 Winter Field Day experience operating as the Everyday Ham Amateur Radio Club using callsign W8EDH. With temperatures plunging to -17°F (-27°C) and dangerous winter storms sweeping the country, we set up at a cabin in the woods with 8 total operators and logged 845 QSOs across multiple bands.

⏱️ TIMESTAMPS
0:00 - Driving to Winter Field Day in -17°F
0:15 - What is Winter Field Day?
0:42 - Why the Everyday Ham Club decided to participate
1:18 - The brutal Michigan weather conditions
2:03 - Jim's Winter Field Day experience
3:09 - Heating problems at the cabin (51°F inside!)
3:53 - James arrives after the heating crisis
4:18 - First time doing a real contest
4:56 - Our antenna setup strategy
5:54 - The 40m monster vertical antenna
6:06 - Antenna walkthrough (40m vertical, Aerial-51 OCFD, Band Hopper)
7:21 - Results: 845 QSOs and contest stats
7:55 - Using World Radio League logger
8:43 - Stats breakdown (110 QSOs in fastest hour, 28 average)
9:24 - Bands we operated (40m, 20m, 80m, 15m, 10m, 2m, 220, 440)
10:06 - Getting CW contacts with Terry W8TMB
10:36 - Multipliers and bonus objectives
11:01 - Running on alternative power (battery vs generator RFI)
11:28 - Winlink email adventure (and the Nigerian prince joke)
12:30 - More multipliers: six bands, multiple modes, continuous operation
12:54 - Objectives we missed (satellites, WFD bulletin, QRP)
13:12 - Total operators (8) and visitors (16+)
13:30 - The W8EDH club callsign
13:44 - Coaching new operators
14:04 - Join our Discord for event info
14:24 - Looking ahead to Summer Field Day & QSO parties
15:06 - Closing montage: Final contacts and packing up

📻 WHAT WE COVERED
- Setting up a multi-operator station in extreme cold
- Running three radios on battery and generator power
- Tips for first-time contesters using World Radio League
- The importance of resonant antennas for contesting
- How to encourage and coach new operators
- Winlink email bonus objective (and the Nigerian prince joke)

snippet.title: Kenwood TM-D750A FCC Filing Reveals All! First Look at the Specs
snippet.description:
The Kenwood TM-D750A is officially real! The FCC filing has been approved and the user guide has been posted online, revealing key specifications for this highly anticipated tri-band mobile radio. Join James (K8JKU), Jim (N8JRD), and Rory (W8KNX) as we break down everything we learned from the FCC documents.

In this episode, we discuss our price predictions (will it be $999, $1299, or even $1500?), the 7-inch display, 220 MHz power output (20 watts!), Wi-Fi and Bluetooth 5.0 capabilities, GPS, D-STAR features, and why the microphone port placement has us scratching our heads.
Plus, we talk tri-band antenna options and whether Rory will actually buy one at Hamcation!

Timestamps:
0:00 - It's not vaporware! FCC filing confirmed
0:49 - Price predictions: What will it cost?
2:03 - Hamcation availability discussion
2:55 - FCC photos breakdown: Ports and connections
4:01 - Heat sink and fan noise concerns
5:26 - MicroSD and USB-C on the head unit
6:20 - Build quality impressions from Hamvention prototype
7:13 - Speaker and audio quality expectations
7:53 - Feature summary: APRS, D-STAR, Wi-Fi
9:27 - Bluetooth 5.0 and wireless standards
10:20 - The big question: 220 MHz power output
12:10 - Tri-band antenna recommendations
13:35 - The mic port placement problem
15:38 - Physical buttons vs touchscreen debate
16:15 - Display visibility in sunlight
17:14 - Diamond CR320B tri-band antenna option
18:52 - Final thoughts: Will Rory buy one?
